skill.name: ai-automation-workflows
skill.description: Build AI automation workflows with inference.sh CLI examples for batch jobs, pipelines, scheduling, monitoring, and Python-based orchestration.
skill.summary: Create AI automation workflows for batch processing, scheduled generation, monitoring, and multi-model pipelines.
skill.author: inference-sh

security_audit.summary: Static findings are largely true positives for command execution, network access, scripts, and persistence-style scheduling guidance. The file appears to be instructional documentation, not confirmed malware, but it includes curl-to-shell installation, cron setup, local file processing, subprocess use, and webhook alerting. Weak cryptography matches appear to be false positives because no cryptographic operation is present at those lines.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped Directly To Shell
high_findings.description: The quick start instructs users to download a remote installer and pipe it directly to sh. This can execute changing remote code with local user privileges before review.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is explicit on the cited line. This is a well-known high-risk installation pattern even when the source domain is legitimate.

high_findings.title: Persistent Scheduled Execution Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill teaches crontab modification and scheduled execution of AI automation scripts. This is legitimate automation, but it creates persistence and recurring network activity.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cron examples are clearly present and are intended to run scripts repeatedly. The context is instructional, so this is high risk rather than confirmed malicious persistence.

medium_findings.title: Command-Heavy Automation Recipes
medium_findings.description: Many examples run shell commands, command substitutions, background jobs, and a Python subprocess wrapper. These are expected for the skill, but they increase execution and injection risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show direct command execution through infsh and subprocess.run. The commands are part of normal workflow examples, so malicious intent is not confirmed.

medium_findings.title: Potential Local Data Exposure To External AI Services
medium_findings.description: The data processing example embeds local file contents into a prompt sent to a remote model. Users could accidentally send sensitive local data without an explicit warning.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example reads files from a local data directory and sends the contents through an infsh app call. The data directory is user-selected, so impact depends on user input.

medium_findings.title: Webhook Alerting Can Leak Command Details
medium_findings.description: The alerting example posts command text and error output to a webhook URL. Error messages and command arguments can contain sensitive workflow data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code sends result and command values to an external webhook. The webhook URL is a placeholder, so this is a disclosure risk rather than confirmed exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External Service References
low_findings.description: The documentation references inference.sh and a placeholder webhook domain. These network references match the skill purpose, but users should verify endpoints before use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in the documentation and are expected for an inference.sh workflow skill. The placeholder webhook is not evidence of a fixed exfiltration endpoint.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptography around description text, prompts, and best-practice bullets. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API use was found at those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows ordinary documentation text and shell prompt examples, not cryptographic code. This strongly supports dismissing the weak-crypto category.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: A remote installer is piped to sh, which executes downloaded code immediately.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern appears on one line and requires no inference.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cron Job Modification
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation instructs users to edit crontab and run automation scripts on recurring schedules.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cron commands are explicit. The surrounding workflow context makes it persistence-like but not inherently malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generic Command Wrapper With Webhook Posting
dangerous_patterns.description: A wrapper runs a provided command, captures output, and posts failure details to a webhook.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wrapper and outbound POST are shown directly. The destination is a placeholder, so the risk depends on user configuration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Python Subprocess Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The Python example invokes the infsh CLI using subprocess.run with caller-provided app identifiers and input data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: subprocess.run is real, but arguments are passed as a list without shell=True. This reduces shell injection risk while preserving external command risk.

content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable AI workflows for content, data, and monitoring tasks. This skill provides patterns for pipelines, schedules, retries, logging, and alerts.
actual_capabilities: Shows batch processing patterns for running the same AI app across many prompts.
Demonstrates sequential pipelines that combine search, writing, image generation, and social content.
Provides examples for retries, fallback models, logging, and error alerts.
Explains cron-based scheduling for daily, weekly, and recurring AI jobs.
Includes a Python subprocess wrapper for invoking inference.sh from scripts.
Shows data file processing patterns that send file content to AI models.
limitations: Examples depend on inference.sh CLI access and valid account authentication.
The skill is documentation only and does not provide tested production scripts.
Shell examples need validation before use with untrusted input or private data.
Webhook, cron, and filesystem paths must be adapted for each environment.

use_cases.title: Plan Recurring Content Generation
use_cases.description: Create a daily or weekly workflow for images, captions, and social content with logging and retry behavior.

use_cases.title: Build Multi-Step AI Pipelines
use_cases.description: Combine research, generation, classification, and post-processing tasks into a repeatable command-line pipeline.

use_cases.title: Process Text Files With AI Models
use_cases.description: Design a batch workflow that reads local text files, sends content to a model, and writes analysis outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Batch Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a simple inference.sh batch workflow for five prompts. Include rate limiting and clear output names.

prompt_templates.title: Add Retry and Logging
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to improve my AI generation script with retries, fallback model selection, structured logging, and safe failure messages.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Scheduled Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan a scheduled AI workflow for weekly reports. Include inputs, outputs, cron timing, alerts, and review checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Advanced Automation System
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my multi-step AI automation design. Identify command, data exposure, scheduling, logging, and monitoring risks.

output_examples.input: Generate a content calendar workflow for five weekdays.
output_examples.output: A weekday plan with topics, model calls, image tasks, caption tasks, output files, and rate limits.

output_examples.input: Add monitoring to a daily AI generation job.
output_examples.output: A workflow outline with log files, status checks, retry rules, failure alerts, and manual review points.

output_examples.input: Process local text files with an AI model.
output_examples.output: A batch processing design that lists input folders, output folders, data review steps, and privacy checks before model submission.

best_practices: Review every command before running examples, especially installers, cron entries, and webhook calls.
Keep secrets and private data out of prompts, logs, filenames, and alert payloads.
Add rate limits, retries, idempotent outputs, and explicit approval steps for scheduled workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers to a shell without inspecting the script first.
Do not schedule AI jobs that can run forever without quotas, alerts, or owner review.
Do not send raw local files to external models without confirming data classification.

faq.question: Does this skill run automation by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow examples and patterns that a user or agent can adapt.

faq.question: What tool does the skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on inference.sh CLI workflows, with shell and Python examples.

faq.question: Can it help with scheduled AI jobs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes cron examples for daily, weekly, and recurring tasks.

faq.question: Is it safe for private data?
faq.answer: Use caution. Some examples send local file content or errors to external services.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The skill includes pipe-to-shell installation, cron setup, subprocess execution, and webhook posting examples.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
